title: Graph-Theoretic Detection of Hilbert Space Fragmentation
authors: A. Rutkowski, M. Mierzejewski, J. Herbrych
abstract: Hilbert-space fragmentation provides a mechanism for ergodicity breaking in quantum many-body systems even in the absence of disorder, leading to dynamically disconnected sectors and strong memory of initial conditions. However, identifying such structures is often challenging and typically relies on prior knowledge of conservation laws or model-specific analytical insight. Here we introduce an unbiased approach based on spectral graph theory and, within this framework, formulate the concept of nearly fragmented systems, in which perturbative processes couple otherwise fragmented sectors while preserving their dynamical imprint. By representing basis states as vertices and Hamiltonian matrix elements as edges, we map the connectivity structure of the many-body Hilbert space onto a graph and analyze it using tools such as the Laplacian spectrum, Fiedler vectors, and modularity. Exact fragmentation corresponds to disconnected graph components, while nearly fragmented systems manifest as weakly connected communities whose structure can still be resolved spectrally. Applying this framework to the one-dimensional $t$-$J$ model and its perturbations, we demonstrate that graph-theoretic diagnostics reliably identify both fragmented and nearly fragmented Hilbert-space structures and capture the hierarchy of dynamical time scales that governs the system's evolution. We further show that the method extends beyond kinetically constrained models by applying it directly to the Hubbard chain, where it reveals the emergence of nearly decoupled subspaces associated with doublon dynamics and spin configurations. Our results establish the spectral graph analysis as a general and scalable tool for diagnosing fragmentation and approximate dynamical constraints in complex quantum many-body systems.
summary: This paper introduces a graph-theoretic framework to detect Hilbert space fragmentation and nearly fragmented regimes in quantum many-body systems. By treating the Hamiltonian as an adjacency matrix, the authors use spectral graph theory to identify disconnected or weakly coupled sectors. The method is shown to be physically accurate, as the identified graph communities correspond to real dynamical timescales in the t-J and Hubbard models.
main_problem: Developing an unbiased method to detect Hilbert space fragmentation and 'nearly fragmented' regimes in quantum many-body systems without requiring prior knowledge of specific conservation laws.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that spectral graph theory (using Laplacian and modularity matrices) can reliably identify both exact and approximate fragmentation, capturing the hierarchical dynamical timescales in the t-J and Hubbard models.
method: The many-body Hilbert space is mapped onto a graph where basis states are vertices and Hamiltonian matrix elements are edges, then analyzed using the Laplacian spectrum, Fiedler vectors, and modularity maximization.
model_or_system: The study focuses on the one-dimensional t-J model (including t-Jz and t-models) and the Hubbard chain, specifically examining regimes near half-filling with varying hole concentrations.
key_observables: Laplacian and modularity eigenvalues, Fiedler vectors, modularity (Q), and the subspace-projected Loschmidt echo (D(tau)).
important_parameters: Spin exchange strength (J/t), number of holes (Nh), system size (L), and the number of rungs in ladder geometries.
main_assumptions: The approach assumes the ability to represent the Hamiltonian in a chosen basis to construct the graph and focuses on the ability to compute matrix elements.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the Laplacian and modularity spectra for different J strengths, the identification of subspaces via eigenvectors, and the time evolution of the projected Loschmidt echo to verify dynamical consistency.
paper_structure: The paper defines the models (Sec II), introduces graph-theoretic tools (Sec III), applies the framework to t-J-like models (Sec IV-V), extends the analysis to the Hubbard chain (Sec VI), and provides mathematical proofs in the Appendices.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it provides a general, scalable tool to diagnose ergodicity breaking and prethermal-like hierarchical timescales in complex quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Circular graph representation of Hamiltonian matrices of (a) fragmented t-Jz model for L = 6 sites, Nh = 2 holes, and Hilbert space dimension dim(H) = 90. Such a system (in Sz tot = 0 magnetization sector) exhibits fragmentation into 6 subspaces. Grey lines depict nonzero matrix elements (connections). (b) The same as (a) but with properly ordered basis states that reveal the Hilbert space fragmentation. (c) Graph of nearly fragmented rung-depleted t-Jz ladder (L = 6, Nh = 2). 4 subspaces detected by Fiedler vector analysis are marked with colors. In (b) and (c), spin configurations in squeezed space are also presented. (d) Graph of nearly fragmented t-J model (L = 6, Nh = 2, and...

Figure 2. Rung-depleted t-Jz ladder. (a) Sketch of the system with R⊥= 2. (b) Exemplary spin configuration for L = 8, Nh = 2, and R⊥= 1. (c) Three exemplary configurations for L = 8, Nh = 2, and R⊥= 2. ”Fixed” q = 2 spins (which cannot be changed by the hole hop) are marked with red and blue colors.

Figure 3. Spectral graph analysis of rung-depleted t-Jz ladder (L = 12, Nh = 2) for various numbers of rungs R⊥= 1, . . . , 6. Panel (a) depicts the first 300 eigenvalues λi of the Laplacian L, while panel (b) depicts the last 300 eigenvalues of modu- larity Q. In the latter, we present eigenvalues in the reversed order, eβi = βdim(H) −βdim(H)+1−i. Note the log-scale on the x-axis. The inset in (b) shows a zoomed-in view of the first few modularity eigenvalues. (c) Sketch of the structure of the Hilbert space for R⊥= 6, 5, 4, 3 (F = 1, 1, 4, 16, respectively).

Figure 4. Lowest eigenvalues (a) λi of the Laplacian L and (b) eβi = βdim(H) −βdim(H)+1−i of the modularity Q, for various system sizes L = 6, 8, 10, 12 and Nh = 2 [with dim(H) = 90, 560, 3150, 16632, respectively] and R⊥= L/2 −1. Note that all considered systems have F = 1. The inset in (b) shows a zoomed-in view of the first few modularity eigenvalues.

Figure 5. Subspace detection based on k-means algorithm (see text for details). Panel (a) illustrates the starting point, i.e., the strictly fragmented system (F = 4 , R⊥= L/2 −2 = 4) for that we put Hα = Cα, (b) the nearly fragmented system (F = 1 , nF = 4 , R⊥= L/2 −1 = 5), and (c) a system for which we don’t see any structure in the spectral analysis of the corresponding graph. In all panels, L = 12 and Nh = 2. See also Fig. 3 for eigenvalues of L and Q. The plots depict two-dimensional histograms of (α, ρ) pairs, where α index corresponds to the theoretically predicted Hα subspace, while ρ corresponds to the detected subspace by the k-means algorithm based on eigenvectors of modularity...
